title: Newbury Street Pop-Up Playbook
purpose: Complete guide for a small business to host a summer pop-up on Newbury Street, Boston — supporting Cyvl demo for Boston CIO Monday news interview

geocode.display_name: Newbury Street, Back Bay, Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts, 02115

blocks.display_name: H&M, 100 Newbury Street, Block B, Back Bay
blocks.notes: Lower numbers — closest to Public Garden, highest foot traffic, most upscale retail character

blocks.display_name: Nike, 200 Newbury Street, Block E, Back Bay
blocks.notes: Mid-street block with major brand activations — Nike Marathon Activation filed here April 2026
Upper Newbury — more accessible rents, local independent businesses, direct T access at Hynes

street_overview.total_length: 8 blocks from Arlington St to Massachusetts Ave
street_overview.character: Boston's premier retail and dining street — brownstone storefronts, sidewalk cafes, designer boutiques, global brands
street_overview.parking: Metered street parking, garages at Prudential and Copley Place nearby

existing_businesses.source: Boston Licensing Board + Inspectional Services Dept — Active as of April 2026

sample_businesses.name: Stephanie's on Newbury
sample_businesses.notes: Iconic Newbury St sidewalk cafe — benchmark for outdoor dining

existing_businesses.business_density_notes: At least 56 active food establishments on Newbury St as of April 2026. High concentration of no-alcohol eateries (40+) and ~15 with beer/wine/full liquor. Recent wave of openings 2024-2026: Venchi, George Howell Coffee, Pink Carrot, Chip City, HEYTEA — signals strong street health. Mix of global brands (Nike, H&M, Google, Adidas) and local independents.

events.name: Run Club and Steak Boston Marathon Celebration
events.notes: Marathon week activation — food + fitness brand tie-in

events.location_description: In-store + Nike owned sidewalk in front of store
events.notes: Brand sidewalk pop-up — key precedent for Cyvl demo: major brands run sidewalk activations here routinely

events.notes: Pride Month — high foot traffic event, World Cup warm-up window

event_licenses_history.pattern_analysis: Newbury St sees a mix of: (1) City-run Open Newbury Sundays (umbrella permit), (2) brand activations tied to major Boston events (Marathon, Pride), (3) cultural/community performances, (4) brand collaboration block parties (CNCPTS x Adidas). Application-to-issue time for successful permits: ~4-6 weeks. Brand activations typically approved faster.

permit_process.overview: A Newbury Street summer pop-up requires 3-5 permits depending on food/alcohol service. Total lead time is 6-8 weeks for a smooth process. File everything simultaneously.

steps.name: Special Event License (Outdoor Public Event)
steps.agency: Mayor's Office of Consumer Affairs and Licensing (MOCAL)
steps.timeline: Apply 30+ days before event (large); 20+ days (small). License issued the week before event.
steps.cost: Varies by event tier — typically $50-$200 for small commercial events
requirements: Completed application signed by Boston Police District Captain
Site plan / event layout
Security plan
Certificate of insurance
Letter from property owner / landlord if on private property

tiers.tier_2: Medium events with road closure or amplified sound
tiers.tier_3: Large events — road races, festivals — most city department involvement

steps.name: ISD Temporary Food Service Permit
steps.timeline: Apply at least 30 days before event
steps.cost: $30 for first day, $5 each additional day (max 14 consecutive days)
requirements: Completed temporary food establishment application
Proof of certified food manager (or current enrollment)
Food handling procedures
Equipment and setup plan
steps.notes: Required for any event selling or serving food. Not required if no food.

steps.name: One-Day Alcohol License (if serving alcohol)
steps.timeline: Apply 3-4 weeks in advance (email preferred for fastest turnaround)
steps.cost: $75/day for malt/wine; $150/day for all-alcohol (nonprofit: reduced rates)
requirements: Application signed by Police Captain in event district
ABCC (Alcoholic Beverages Control Commission) application
Certificate of Inspection from ISD
Fire Assembly Permit (for 49+ capacity events)
Property owner/landlord permission
Site plan showing alcohol service area, egresses
Security plan
Must purchase alcohol from ABCC-approved wholesaler (not package stores)

steps.name: Outdoor Dining / Sidewalk Cafe Permit (if using public sidewalk space)
steps.agency: City of Boston — Public Works + Licensing Board
steps.timeline: Seasonal program runs May 1 - October 31 annually. Apply before season start.
steps.cost: $399/month with liquor license; $199/month without (public property only; private property = free)
requirements: Business must have existing food license
ADA-compliant layout — must maintain 5-foot clear pedestrian passage
Application via boston.gov portal
Bollards or delineators may be required
Liquor license required to serve alcohol outdoors
steps.notes: For a pure pop-up (not attached to an existing licensed business), this permit chain differs — use Special Event path instead.

steps.name: Tent / Temporary Structure Permit
steps.timeline: File with other permits — typically 2-3 weeks
steps.cost: Varies — check fire department fee schedule
requirements: Required for any tent over a threshold size
Key exception: 4 or fewer 10x10 tents touching without sides = NO permit needed
Larger aggregations need permit
Stamped engineer/architect approval if capacity over 199

steps.name: Generator Permit
requirements: Generator permit application
Fuel storage documentation
Location plan showing generator placement relative to tents/structures
steps.notes: Required for most outdoor events using portable power.

steps.name: One-Time Entertainment License (if live music or amplified sound)
requirements: Application with signed police captain approval
Event details, times, security plan
May require noise management plan

steps.name: Transient Vendor License (Massachusetts State — if selling goods)
steps.agency: Massachusetts Secretary of State / City Clerk
requirements: Required if selling retail goods from a temporary location
Does not apply to established brick-and-mortar doing an outdoor extension

permit_process.total_timeline: 6-8 weeks from first application to event day (file all permits simultaneously, not sequentially)
permit_process.total_cost_permits: $300-$700 for a 1-day non-alcohol food pop-up; $600-$1,200 for alcohol-serving event

insurance_requirements.additional_insured: City of Boston must be named as additional insured
insurance_requirements.notes: Certificate of insurance required with special event application. Contact local event insurance broker or use providers like Protector Plans or SBC Insurance.

permit_process.boston_police_district: District D-4 covers Back Bay / Newbury Street. Application must be signed by District D-4 Captain before submission to MOCAL.

infrastructure_notes.cyvl_data_availability: Cyvl project 090e18f2-0002-4a70-90b4-8f073d26294d covers Somerville, MA — NOT Boston/Newbury Street. No direct Cyvl infrastructure data is available for Newbury St in this project.
infrastructure_notes.imagery_status: Imagery search returned 94 results for 'storefront sidewalk' and 19 for 'sidewalk cafe outdoor dining' but ALL located in Somerville, MA (scan_1185_SOMERVILLE_MA). Coordinates confirm ~42.37-42.40N / 71.07-71.13W = Somerville, not Back Bay (42.348-42.351N / 71.075-71.093W). No Newbury St imagery in this project.

representative_imagery.note: Somerville imagery can serve as representative example of Boston-area street-level infrastructure data. Shows sidewalk cafes with chairs, umbrellas, dining tables detected by Cyvl AI.

best_demo_images.note: Sidewalk cafe scene — most relevant for demo
Active street scene with outdoor dining elements
Outdoor dining with umbrellas and tables detected

infrastructure_notes.sidewalk_width: Newbury Street sidewalks are approximately 15-20 feet wide — sufficient for pop-up setup plus 5-foot ADA pedestrian clearance requirement
infrastructure_notes.street_surface: Brick and asphalt — historic brownstone character. Tent staking not recommended; use weighted bases.
infrastructure_notes.utilities: Power outlets not available on public sidewalk — generator or battery required for any powered equipment
infrastructure_notes.open_newbury_sundays: 10 Sundays July 13 - Sept 21 (2025 schedule; 2026 TBD). Cars banned Berkeley to Mass Ave 10am-8pm. Best pop-up days — 50,000+ pedestrians.

imagery_notes: Cyvl project 090e18f2-0002-4a70-90b4-8f073d26294d is Somerville-only. Newbury St / Back Bay Boston not in this Cyvl dataset. Somerville imagery available as representative demo material for Boston CIO presentation — shows Cyvl's ability to detect sidewalk cafes, outdoor dining infrastructure, and pavement scores at street level.

setup_checklist.item: Identify target date — Open Newbury Sunday for maximum foot traffic (50K+ pedestrians)
Choose block — Mid (100-200 blocks) for brand visibility; Upper (300s) for lower competition
Secure property owner permission for any private sidewalk/storefront use
File Special Event License with MOCAL (signed by D-4 Police Captain)
File ISD Temporary Food Service Permit (if selling/serving food)
File One-Day Alcohol License with Licensing Board (if serving alcohol)
File tent/temporary structure permit with Fire Prevention (if tent > 4x 10x10)
File generator permit with Fire Prevention
Obtain event liability insurance ($1M+ general liability; name City of Boston as additional insured)
File One-Time Entertainment License if live music or amplified sound
Obtain Massachusetts Transient Vendor License if selling physical retail goods
Rent tent(s) — up to 4x 10x10 no-sides tents require no permit
Order signage, banners, branded display fixtures
Arrange power (generator rental + fuel)
Hire staff (2-3 minimum for busy Open Newbury Sunday)
Set up POS system / Square / contactless payments
Confirm food sourcing / catering partnerships (if pop-up restaurant)
Contact Newbury Street League for co-promotion opportunities
Load-in day-of: weighted tent anchors (NO staking into brick), fire extinguisher on site
Maintain 5-foot ADA pedestrian clearance on sidewalk at all times

equipment_rental.tent_10x10_per_unit: $100-$200/day (Boston-area vendors: Party Time Events, Flying Cow, Baystate Tent)

space_rental.open_newbury_public_space: Free during Open Newbury Sundays (city closes street — public space)

food_and_product.notes: Variable — product cost/COGS not included. For food pop-up, typical food cost ratio is 28-35% of revenue.

total_estimated_1_day_pop_up.minimum_no_frills: $1,500-$2,500 (2 tents, food permit, liability insurance, 2 staff, minimal signage)
total_estimated_1_day_pop_up.typical_small_brand: $4,000-$8,000 (professional setup, branded signage, generator, food, alcohol, 3 staff)

best_blocks.why: Highest foot traffic (closest to Public Garden and Arlington T stop), premium brand neighbors (H&M, J.Crew vicinity), most photogenic brownstone facades. Best for fashion, lifestyle, or high-end food brands.
best_blocks.trade_offs: Highest competition for permits, most scrutiny, property owners more selective

best_blocks.why: Sweet spot — still extremely high foot traffic, major brand presence (Nike at 200, Google at 153, Adidas activations at 18 area), proven brand activation territory. Nike Marathon Activation filed here. Mid-day sun exposure excellent.
best_blocks.trade_offs: Competitive — Nike/Google/Adidas own their sidewalk space, need to find free frontage

best_blocks.why: Direct Hynes/ICA T stop access, lower rent for private space rental, more independent business character, less corporate competition, good for authentic/indie brand pop-ups. George Howell and Pink Carrot just opened at 400.
best_blocks.trade_offs: Slightly lower foot traffic vs. lower Newbury, but still 15,000-20,000/day

foot_traffic_data.open_newbury_coverage: All 8 blocks from Berkeley St to Massachusetts Ave
sources: City of Boston traffic advisory (Open Newbury August 2022 cited 50K)
foot_traffic_data.peak_times: Weekends noon-6pm, Sundays especially strong due to Open Newbury program
foot_traffic_data.visitor_mix: Local residents, students (BU, Northeastern, Berklee nearby), professionals, international tourists, FIFA World Cup 2026 international visitors (June-July 2026)

world_cup_angle.summary: FIFA World Cup 2026 is a massive multiplier for any Newbury Street pop-up running June-July 2026. Boston hosts 7 matches (including a Quarterfinal) from June 13 to July 9 at Gillette Stadium. The City's official Fan Festival is at City Hall Plaza — but Back Bay is a prime overflow zone.
world_cup_angle.boston_matches: 7 matches total including Quarterfinal — June 13 to July 9, 2026
world_cup_angle.official_fan_zone: City Hall Plaza (free, tournament-long, giant screens, food/entertainment)
world_cup_angle.back_bay_opportunity: Back Bay recommended for international fans — close to hotels, T access, premium retail/dining. Expect significant spillover foot traffic on match days.
pop_up_tie_in_ideas: FIFA-themed merchandise pop-up (officially licensed or country-flag gear)
International food pop-up featuring cuisine from participating nations
Live match viewing setup with outdoor screen (entertainment permit required)
National team supporter merchandise and gear
Cultural exchange pop-up celebrating fan cultures (no merchandise license needed)

key_dates_for_planning.peak_pop_up_window: June 13 - July 9, 2026 — Open Newbury Sundays overlap (July 13 starts, but pre-Open Newbury weekends in June are strong too)

world_cup_angle.permit_implications: Apply for permits in April 2026 for June-July events. World Cup period may see heightened city scrutiny — file early. Consider multi-day or multi-Sunday permit (up to 14 consecutive days under temp food permit).
world_cup_angle.cyvl_demo_angle: Cyvl infrastructure intelligence shows city planners exactly where sidewalk capacity exists, pavement conditions for safe crowd flow, and sign inventory for wayfinding — all critical for managing the 100K+ international visitors flowing through Back Bay during the tournament. This is the story: infrastructure visibility for event-ready cities.
world_cup_angle.funding_note: As of April 2026, Boston faces $8M federal funding shortfall for World Cup preparations — local business activations and private-sector pop-ups become more important, not less, for fan experience.

newbury_street_league.name: Newbury Street League
newbury_street_league.role: Non-profit business association representing Newbury Street commercial district
newbury_street_league.description: Community of shop owners, restaurateurs, and creatives. Co-organizes events, advocates for street improvements, promotes Open Newbury.

back_bay_association.name: Back Bay Association
back_bay_association.role: Broader Back Bay neighborhood business advocacy

business_associations.notes: Both organizations are key relationships for any pop-up operator — they have institutional knowledge of permit process, relationships with city officials, and can co-promote events.
